did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses machine learning and deep learning models for melanoma detection. Specifically, it employs a stacked modeling approach combining base machine learning models with deep learning models (using transfer learning from ImageNet) to classify skin lesions as benign or malignant using the ISIC 2018 dataset. It also utilizes SHAP (Shapley Additive exPlanations) for interpretability.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and evaluation of a stacked modeling approach for melanoma detection. It describes specific methods including data preprocessing, feature extraction, machine learning models, deep learning models with transfer learning, and SHAP for interpretability. It reports results using metrics like accuracy, F1-score, Cohen's kappa, and AUC-ROC on the ISIC 2018 dataset. Although published in a conference proceedings (ICAICCIT), it contains original research results rather than being a narrative or systematic review.

title: Interpretable Melanoma Detection Utilizing Stacked Modeling Approach
journal: 2023 International Conference on Advances in Computation, Communication and Information Technology (ICAICCIT)
brief_summary: This study addresses the challenge of melanoma detection by developing an interpretable stacked modeling approach using both machine learning and deep learning techniques. The researchers utilized a balanced subset of the ISIC 2018 dataset containing 3,297 dermoscopic images classified as benign or malignant. The methodology involved training base machine learning models with hand-crafted features and deep learning models via transfer learning from ImageNet, which were then combined using a stacking ensemble strategy. Model performance was evaluated using accuracy, F1-score, Cohen's kappa, and AUC-ROC metrics, with statistical significance assessed via p-values. The results indicated that deep learning-based solutions were more reliable, with the top-ranked ensemble model demonstrating high precision and recall. Additionally, SHAP (Shapley Additive exPlanations) was employed to generate heatmaps, enhancing the interpretability of the model's predictions for clinical utility.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the two classes in the balanced subset of the ISIC 2018 dataset used for experiments. The dataset contained 1197 malignant images in the training set and 300 in the test set. The study explicitly excluded other disease categories from the ISIC 2018 dataset to focus on melanoma detection.

One of the two classes in the balanced subset of the ISIC 2018 dataset used for experiments. The dataset contained 1440 benign images in the training set and 360 in the test set. These were used as the negative class for melanoma detection.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of skin lesions into benign or malignant categories
all_task_types.details: The paper focuses on classifying dermoscopic images from the ISIC 2018 dataset into two discrete classes: benign and malignant. The study explicitly states that the dataset was balanced to contain only these two classifications, excluding other disease categories. The models predict discrete diagnostic labels (benign vs. malignant) using metrics such as accuracy, F1-score, Cohen's kappa, and AUC-ROC.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A curated, balanced subset of the ISIC 2018 challenge dataset containing only benign and malignant skin lesion images. The original dataset had seven classes, but this study purposefully excluded other disease categories to balance the class disparity and focus on melanoma detection. The images are dermoscopic photos with dimensions of 224x224 pixels.

use.training: Used to train base machine learning models with hand-crafted features and deep learning models via transfer learning from ImageNet. Predictions from base models were used for level-one stacking via 5-fold cross-validation.
use.testing: Used to evaluate the performance of the stacked ensemble models using metrics such as accuracy, F1-score, Cohen's kappa, and AUC-ROC.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: Used to carry out stacking on the training set, where predictions from base models serve as input for the level 1 model.

Used to sample photos from the original ISIC 2018 challenge collection to create a balanced dataset, ensuring a proportional number of samples were taken from each stratum (benign and malignant classes).
Used to see if there are statistically significant differences in the performance of the various models under study by contrasting results using null and alternative hypotheses.
Used to evaluate statistical significance, with p-values less than 0.05 considered significant.
Used as an assessment indicator to gauge the degree of agreement between genuine labels and predictions.

hypothesis_testing.test: Statistical hypothesis testing (specific name not provided, but p-values were calculated to compare model performances)
hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There are no statistically significant differences in the performance of the various models under study.
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: There are statistically significant differences in the performance of the various models under study.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: P-values less than 0.05 were used to evaluate statistical significance, indicating that differences in model performance were considered statistically significant if the p-value was below this threshold.

ISIC 2018 (Balanced Subset).description: A balanced subset of the ISIC 2018 dataset containing 3,297 dermoscopic images (2,637 training, 660 testing) classified as benign or malignant.

ISIC 2018 (Balanced Subset).notes: The paper states that deep learning-based solutions were more reliable and successful. The top-ranked ensemble model demonstrated great precision and recall. The study utilized Cohen's kappa, accuracy, F1-score, and AUC-ROC as evaluation metrics. Statistical significance was assessed using p-values (< 0.05). Specific numerical values for these metrics for the proposed models are not provided in the supplied text chunks, though the methodology for their calculation is described.

architecture_used.main_model: Stacked Modeling Approach (Ensemble of Base Machine Learning Models and Deep Learning Models)
architecture_used.pretraining: Deep learning models were pre-trained on ImageNet data for transfer learning.

architecture_details.base_models: Classical machine learning models trained on hand-crafted features (form, texture, color) and deep learning models (CNNs) trained via transfer learning.
architecture_details.stacking_method: Level-one stacking using 5-fold cross-validation on the training set. Predictions from base models served as input for the level 1 model.
architecture_details.deep_learning_backbone: Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) with automatic feature retrieval from ImageNet-pretrained models.
architecture_details.interpretability_module: SHAP (Shapley Additive exPlanations) used to generate heatmaps highlighting areas suggestive of melanoma.

training_details.dataset: Balanced subset of ISIC 2018 (3,297 images: 2,637 training, 660 testing).
training_details.preprocessing: Elimination of hair, shading, and glares; segmentation; feature extraction; normalization/scaling of images.
training_details.sampling: Stratified probability sampling (randomization) to balance classes.

classification_or_output_head.output_description: Predictions from base models combined via stacking ensemble strategy.

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Classical machine learning models (trained on hand-crafted features)
Deep learning models (CNNs with transfer learning from ImageNet)
Stacked ensemble model (Level-one stacking of base machine learning and deep learning models)
preprocessing_techniques: Elimination of hair, shading, and glares (artifact removal)
Random selection of images to create a balanced dataset (stratified sampling)
Exclusion of non-melanoma disease categories from the ISIC 2018 dataset

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Used to sample photos from the original ISIC 2018 challenge collection to create a balanced dataset, ensuring a proportional number of samples were taken from each stratum (benign and malignant classes). This was used to address class disparity rather than traditional geometric/color augmentation.
Elimination of hair, shading, and glares from dermoscopic images during preprocessing to reduce occlusions and noise, as referenced in the literature review regarding data filtering techniques.
Images were scaled to 224x224 pixels and normalized to ensure consistent input sizes for the models, preventing learning delays and instability.

conclusions: The study developed an interpretable stacked modeling approach combining base machine learning models with deep learning models (using transfer learning from ImageNet) to classify skin lesions as benign or malignant.
Deep learning-based solutions were found to be more reliable and successful compared to other approaches, with the top-ranked ensemble model demonstrating high precision and recall.

The use of SHAP (Shapley Additive exPlanations) to generate heatmaps successfully emphasized areas of the images suggestive of melanoma, thereby improving the interpretability of the model's predictions for clinical utility.
The proposed fusion strategy is not intended to replace dermatologists' knowledge but to assist healthcare practitioners in detecting probable instances through automated advanced scanning.

The authors acknowledge limitations regarding the current dataset size and suggest that future work should focus on developing more effective data purification techniques to remove occlusions and accessing larger, balanced datasets to achieve even better melanoma detection accuracy.
important_remarks: The study uses a significantly reduced subset of the ISIC 2018 dataset (3,297 images) created by excluding all non-melanoma classes to balance the data, which limits the generalizability of the model to multi-class skin lesion classification.
The dataset creation process used an unblinded stratified sampling approach, which may introduce selection bias compared to standard blinded randomization protocols.

The paper does not report specific numerical performance metrics (e.g., exact accuracy, F1-score, or AUC values) for the proposed stacked model, relying instead on qualitative descriptions such as 'outstanding degree of accuracy' and 'great precision'.
The dataset size is acknowledged as a limitation by the authors, who note that accessing larger, balanced datasets is necessary for future work to improve detection accuracy.

The study focuses exclusively on binary classification (benign vs. malignant) and does not address the clinical challenge of distinguishing melanoma from other specific benign lesions (e.g., seborrheic keratosis) which are common sources of false positives in dermatology.

The proposed fusion strategy is explicitly stated to be an assistive tool for healthcare practitioners rather than a replacement for dermatologists' knowledge, highlighting a need for clinical validation before deployment.
